verses.text: And it came to pass in the ninth year of his reign, in the tenth month, in the tenth day of the month, that Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came, he, and all his host, against Jerusalem, and pitched against it; and they built forts against it round about. And the city was besieged unto the eleventh year of king Zedekiah. And on the ninth day of the fourth month the famine prevailed in the city, and there was no bread for the people of the land. Guidance: The final siege begins. Zedekiah's rebellion (24:20) brings the full weight of Babylon down upon Jerusalem. The narrator records the date with solemn precision — the tenth day of the tenth month of Zedekiah's ninth year (a date later commemorated as a fast, Zech 8:19; Ezek 24:1-2) — when Nebuchadnezzar and his whole army encircle the city and build siege-works around it. The siege lasts about eighteen months, 'unto the eleventh year of king Zedekiah,' and the slow horror unfolds: 'the famine prevailed in the city, and there was no bread for the people of the land.' The chapter is showing us the beginning of the end — the catastrophe the prophets had warned of for generations, now arriving in grim, dated reality. The careful dating tells us this is sober history, the fulfillment of repeated prophetic warning. The chapter is teaching us the terrible patience and certainty of judgment long delayed: the LORD bore with Judah through centuries of idolatry, sent prophet after prophet, gave reformations and reprieves, but when the appointed time came, the judgment fell with relentless precision. The famine that 'prevailed in the city' is the covenant curse of siege (Deut 28:52-53; Lev 26:25-26) coming to pass exactly as warned. The chapter is teaching us that there is a real and dreadful end to the time of God's forbearance — that warnings unheeded eventually become judgments executed, dated and certain. Jerusalem, the chosen city, the place of the temple where the LORD set His name, is now ringed with the siege-works of a pagan army, starving within its walls. The long story of the kings, with all its mercies and warnings, has come to this.

And the city was broken up, and all the men of war fled by night by the way of the gate between two walls, which is by the king's garden: (now the Chaldees were against the city round about:) and the king went the way toward the plain. And the army of the Chaldees pursued after the king, and overtook him in the plains of Jericho: and all his army were scattered from him. So they took the king, and brought him up to the king of Babylon to Riblah; and they gave judgment upon him. And they slew the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters of brass, and carried him to Babylon. Guidance: The capture and blinding of Zedekiah. The wall is breached; Zedekiah and his soldiers attempt a desperate night escape through the gate by the king's garden, fleeing toward the Jordan plain. But the Babylonians pursue and overtake the king 'in the plains of Jericho,' his army scattering in flight. He is captured and brought to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah, where 'they gave judgment upon him' — and the sentence is horrific: 'they slew the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters of brass, and carried him to Babylon.' The last thing the king ever saw was the execution of his sons; then his eyes were put out, and he was dragged blind and in chains to Babylon, to die there. The chapter is showing us the wretched end of the last king of Judah — and the fulfillment of two prophecies that had seemed contradictory but met precisely here: Jeremiah had said Zedekiah would be taken to Babylon and see the king of Babylon eye to eye (Jer 32:4; 34:3), while Ezekiel had said he would be brought to Babylon yet 'not see it' though he would die there (Ezek 12:13). Both came true to the letter: Zedekiah saw Nebuchadnezzar's face at Riblah, then was blinded before being carried on to Babylon, which he never saw. The chapter is teaching us, once again, the exact fulfillment of God's word, even in details that seemed impossible to reconcile. The chapter is teaching us the bitter harvest of refusing the LORD's word: had Zedekiah heeded Jeremiah and submitted to Babylon, the city and his family might have been spared (Jer 38:17-18); his faithless rebellion brought the slaughter of his sons before his eyes and his own blind captivity. The chapter is teaching us the dreadful cost of a hardened heart that will not heed God's clear word in the day of opportunity — the last sight of a king who would not see spiritually was the death of his sons, and then darkness.

And in the fifth month, on the seventh day of the month, which is the nineteenth year of king Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, came Nebuzaradan, captain of the guard, a servant of the king of Babylon, unto Jerusalem: And he burnt the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem, and every great man's house burnt he with fire. And all the army of the Chaldees, that were with the captain of the guard, brake down the walls of Jerusalem round about. Now the rest of the people that were left in the city, and the fugitives that fell away to the king of Babylon, with the remnant of the multitude, did Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard carry away. But the captain of the guard left of the poor of the land to be vinedressers and husbandmen. Guidance: The burning of the temple and the city. The final, irreversible blow falls. About a month after the city's capture, Nebuzaradan, captain of Nebuchadnezzar's guard, arrives and carries out the destruction: 'he burnt the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem.' The temple of Solomon — the house where the LORD had set His name, the center of Israel's worship for nearly four hundred years — is burned to the ground. The royal palace, every great house, the whole city goes up in flames; the walls are broken down. The surviving population and the deserters are carried away to Babylon; only 'the poor of the land' are left as vinedressers and farmers. The chapter is showing us the unthinkable: the destruction of the temple itself. For centuries the people had presumed that Jerusalem and the temple were inviolable — 'the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD' (Jer 7:4) — as if God's presence guaranteed the building's safety regardless of their sin. Now the temple burns. The chapter is teaching us the most sobering lesson of the entire book: that the LORD will not be held hostage to a building, and that He will abandon even the place where He put His name when His people persist in defiling it. The presence of the temple never made the people safe while their hearts were far from God; in the end, the house they had filled with idols (ch 21) was given to the fire. The chapter is teaching us that no sacred place, no religious heritage, no covenant privilege can substitute for a faithful heart — and that the LORD takes no pleasure in mere buildings when the worship within them has become corrupt. (This is the catastrophe Lamentations mourns, and the loss that hangs over the exiles by the rivers of Babylon, Ps 137.) The chapter is teaching us, too, the LORD's continued mercy even in judgment: He left 'the poor of the land' in the land — a remnant, the seed of a future return. But the great loss is total: the city, the palace, and above all the temple, are gone. This is the lowest point of the entire Old Testament narrative — the visible end of the kingdom the LORD had built through David and Solomon.

And the pillars of brass that were in the house of the LORD, and the bases, and the brasen sea that was in the house of the LORD, did the Chaldees break in pieces, and carried the brass of them to Babylon. And the pots, and the shovels, and the snuffers, and the spoons, and all the vessels of brass wherewith they ministered, took they away. And the firepans, and the bowls, and such things as were of gold, in gold, and of silver, in silver, the captain of the guard took away. The two pillars, one sea, and the bases which Solomon had made for the house of the LORD; the brass of all these vessels was without weight. The height of the one pillar was eighteen cubits, and the chapiter upon it was brass: and the height of the chapiter three cubits; and the wreathen work, and pomegranates upon the chapiter round about, all of brass: and like unto these had the second pillar with wreathen work. Guidance: The temple furnishings carried away. The narrator lingers, almost mournfully, over the inventory of the temple's sacred objects now being broken up and hauled to Babylon: the great bronze pillars (Jachin and Boaz), the wheeled bronze bases, the bronze 'sea,' the pots, shovels, snuffers, spoons, firepans, bowls — everything of gold taken as gold, everything of silver as silver. The narrator pauses to recall their glory: 'the two pillars, one sea, and the bases which Solomon had made for the house of the LORD; the brass of all these vessels was without weight' (too vast to measure), and describes the magnificent pillars — eighteen cubits high, crowned with ornate bronze capitals wreathed with pomegranates. The chapter is showing us the dismantling of Solomon's glorious temple piece by piece — the objects that 1 Kings 7 had described being made with such care and wonder are now described again, in the same loving detail, as they are smashed and carried off. The repetition is deliberate and elegiac: the narrator wants us to feel the full weight of the loss by recalling exactly what is being destroyed. The chapter is teaching us, by this careful catalog of loss, the depth of what sin finally costs. The pillars Solomon raised to stand 'for ever,' the bronze sea, the precious vessels — all the beauty that generations of Israelites had worshipped before — reduced to scrap metal and trophy for a pagan king. And the pots, and the shovels, and the snuffers, and the spoons, and all the vessels of brass wherewith they ministered, took they away. And the firepans, and the bowls, and such things as were of gold, in gold, and of silver, in silver, the captain of the guard took away. And the captain of the guard took Seraiah the chief priest, and Zephaniah the second priest, and the three keepers of the door: And out of the city he took an officer that was set over the men of war, and five men of them that were in the king's presence, which were found in the city, and the principal scribe of the host, which mustered the people of the land, and threescore men of the people of the land that were found in the city: And Nebuzaradan captain of the guard took these, and brought them to the king of Babylon to Riblah: And the king of Babylon smote them, and slew them at Riblah in the land of Hamath. So Judah was carried away out of their land. Guidance: The execution of the leaders — 'So Judah was carried away out of their land.' Nebuzaradan rounds up the leadership that remained: Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the deputy priest, the three temple doorkeepers, a senior military officer, five royal counselors, the chief recruiting scribe, and sixty men of the people. These are taken to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah, where 'the king of Babylon smote them, and slew them.' The civil, military, and religious leadership of Judah is executed. Then the narrator's heavy, climactic summary statement: 'So Judah was carried away out of their land.' The chapter is showing us the formal, final end of the kingdom of Judah. The northern kingdom had fallen in 722 BC (ch 17); now, in 586 BC, the southern kingdom of Judah follows it into exile. The phrase 'So Judah was carried away out of their land' is the dreadful counterpart to 17:23 ('So was Israel carried away out of their own land'). Both kingdoms are now gone; the entire nation that the LORD brought into the land under Joshua has been removed from it. The chapter is teaching us the completeness of the judgment: not only the king and the temple, but the whole leadership class — priests, officers, counselors, scribes — is destroyed, and the people are gone from the land. The covenant warning of Deuteronomy 28:63-64 ('ye shall be plucked from off the land... and the LORD shall scatter thee among all people') is now fully realized. The chapter is teaching us the most solemn truth of the books of Kings, here stated with terrible finality: a people who would not have the LORD as their God, who filled His land and His house with idols and innocent blood despite centuries of patient warning, are at last 'carried away out of their land.' The land the LORD gave is lost; the people He planted are uprooted. The narrative that began with the glory of Solomon's kingdom ends with Judah in chains, marching to Babylon. Yet even here, the books of Kings will not leave us in total darkness — the final paragraph holds one small, stubborn ember of hope.

And as for the people that remained in the land of Judah, whom Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had left, even over them he made Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan, ruler. And when all the captains of the armies, they and their men, heard that the king of Babylon had made Gedaliah governor, there came to Gedaliah to Mizpah, even Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, and Johanan the son of Careah, and Seraiah the son of Tanhumeth the Netophathite, and Jaazaniah the son of a Maachathite, they and their men. And Gedaliah sware to them, and to their men, and said unto them, Fear not to be the servants of the Chaldees: dwell in the land, and serve the king of Babylon; and it shall be well with you. Guidance: Gedaliah's brief governorship. Babylon does not leave the land entirely empty; over the remnant of the poor who remain, Nebuchadnezzar appoints Gedaliah son of Ahikam (grandson of Shaphan the scribe of Josiah's reformation, 22:3 — from a godly, faithful family) as governor, based at Mizpah. The scattered Judean army captains and their men gather to him. Gedaliah, a wise and godly leader, gives them sound counsel under oath: 'Fear not to be the servants of the Chaldees: dwell in the land, and serve the king of Babylon; and it shall be well with you.' He urges submission to Babylon and peaceful settlement — exactly the counsel Jeremiah had given all along (Jer 27:11-12; and Jeremiah himself joined Gedaliah's community, Jer 40). The chapter is showing us a glimmer of order and hope amid the ruins. A remnant remains in the land under a godly governor who offers the path of peace: accept the consequences of the judgment, submit to Babylon, dwell in the land, and there is yet a future. The chapter is teaching us that even after catastrophic judgment, the LORD provides a way forward for the humble who will accept His chastening and live in submission rather than continued rebellion. Gedaliah's counsel echoes the prophets: the judgment cannot be undone, but a life of quiet faithfulness under it can yet be blessed ('it shall be well with you'). The chapter is teaching us the wisdom of accepting God's discipline rather than fighting it — the remnant's hope lay not in renewed rebellion but in submission and patient endurance. Gedaliah represents the path the whole nation should have taken: humble acceptance of the LORD's chastening hand. Tragically, as the next verses show, even this small hope would be shattered by yet more violence — but for a moment, in the wreckage, there was a godly leader and a word of peace.

But it came to pass in the seventh month, that Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, the son of Elishama, of the seed royal, came, and ten men with him, and smote Gedaliah, that he died, and the Jews and the Chaldees that were with him at Mizpah. And all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the armies, arose, and came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees. Guidance: The murder of Gedaliah and the flight to Egypt. The fragile hope is destroyed by yet another act of violence. Ishmael son of Nethaniah, 'of the seed royal' (a member of the Davidic family, likely resentful that a non-royal governed, and incited by the Ammonite king per Jer 40:14), comes with ten men and assassinates Gedaliah, along with the Jews and Babylonian soldiers with him at Mizpah. (This murder, on top of the fall of Jerusalem, was so grievous that it too became a commemorative fast, Zech 7:5; 8:19 — 'the fast of the seventh month.') In the terror that follows, 'all the people... came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees.' Fearing Babylonian reprisal for the murder of their appointed governor, the remnant flees to Egypt — taking the prophet Jeremiah with them against his will (Jer 43:1-7). The chapter is showing us the bitter self-destruction of the remnant. The one path of hope — quiet submission under godly Gedaliah — is shattered by a Davidic prince's jealous violence, and the survivors flee to the very Egypt the prophets had always condemned them for trusting. The chapter is teaching us the tragic persistence of Judah's old sins even in the wreckage: a son of David murders the LORD's appointed governor, and the people return to Egypt — undoing the exodus, fleeing back to the land of bondage from which the LORD had once redeemed them. The chapter is teaching us that judgment does not automatically produce repentance: even after the unimaginable catastrophe of 586 BC, the remnant repeated the nation's characteristic sins — violence, distrust of God's word, and flight to Egypt. The chapter is teaching us the depth of human stubbornness — that a people can pass through the fire of judgment and still not learn to trust and obey. The flight to Egypt is the narrative's final picture of the people in the land: not a chastened remnant clinging to the LORD, but frightened survivors fleeing back to Egypt. The land is now effectively empty. The story of the kingdom in the land is over.

But it came to pass in the seventh month, that Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, the son of Elishama, of the seed royal, came, and ten men with him, and smote Gedaliah, that he died, and the Jews and the Chaldees that were with him at Mizpah. And all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the armies, arose, and came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees. Guidance: The murder of Gedaliah and the flight to Egypt. The fragile hope is destroyed by yet another act of violence. Ishmael son of Nethaniah, 'of the seed royal' (a member of the Davidic family, likely resentful that a non-royal governed, and incited by the Ammonite king per Jer 40:14), comes with ten men and assassinates Gedaliah, along with the Jews and Babylonian soldiers with him at Mizpah. (This murder, on top of the fall of Jerusalem, was so grievous that it too became a commemorative fast, Zech 7:5; 8:19 — 'the fast of the seventh month.') In the terror that follows, 'all the people... came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees.' Fearing Babylonian reprisal for the murder of their appointed governor, the remnant flees to Egypt — taking the prophet Jeremiah with them against his will (Jer 43:1-7). The chapter is showing us the bitter self-destruction of the remnant. The one path of hope — quiet submission under godly Gedaliah — is shattered by a Davidic prince's jealous violence, and the survivors flee to the very Egypt the prophets had always condemned them for trusting. The chapter is teaching us the tragic persistence of Judah's old sins even in the wreckage: a son of David murders the LORD's appointed governor, and the people return to Egypt — undoing the exodus, fleeing back to the land of bondage from which the LORD had once redeemed them. The chapter is teaching us that judgment does not automatically produce repentance: even after the unimaginable catastrophe of 586 BC, the remnant repeated the nation's characteristic sins — violence, distrust of God's word, and flight to Egypt. The chapter is teaching us the depth of human stubbornness — that a people can pass through the fire of judgment and still not learn to trust and obey. The flight to Egypt is the narrative's final picture of the people in the land: not a chastened remnant clinging to the LORD, but frightened survivors fleeing back to Egypt. The land is now effectively empty. The story of the kingdom in the land is over.

And it came to pass in the seven and thirtieth year of the captivity of Jehoiachin king of Judah, in the twelfth month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, that Evilmerodach king of Babylon in the year that he began to reign did lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison; And he spake kindly to him, and set his throne above the throne of the kings that were with him in Babylon; And changed his prison garments: and he did eat bread continually before him all the days of his life. And his allowance was a continual allowance given him of the king, a daily rate for every day, all the days of his life. Guidance: The ember of hope — Jehoiachin lifted up. The books of Kings end not in total darkness but with a small, deliberate ember of hope. Thirty-seven years into the exile, a new Babylonian king, Evil-merodach (son of Nebuchadnezzar), 'did lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison' — the Davidic king who had been carried to Babylon in 597 BC is released from prison, spoken to kindly, given a throne above the other captive kings, his prison clothes exchanged for royal garments, and a daily allowance for the rest of his life. The chapter is showing us, in this final scene, that the lamp of David was not utterly extinguished. The temple is burned, the city ruined, the people scattered — yet a son of David lives, honored at a foreign king's table, and the royal line continues. The narrator ends the long, dark history on this note for a reason. The chapter is teaching us that even in the deepest judgment, the LORD has not forgotten His covenant with David (2 Sam 7) — the promise of an enduring Davidic line survives the exile in the person of Jehoiachin, who appears in the genealogy of Christ (Matt 1:11-12). The lifting up of the king's head is a quiet sign that God's promises are not annulled by His people's failure; the dynasty through which Messiah would come is preserved, even in captivity. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's last word over His people, even in exile, is not abandonment but a thread of hope — the kindness shown to Jehoiachin foreshadows the future restoration (the return under Cyrus, the rebuilding of the temple) and, ultimately, the coming of the Son of David in whom all the promises find their yes. The books of Kings, which have traced Israel's long descent from Solomon's glory to Babylon's chains, close with a captive Davidic king eating bread at the conqueror's table — a small mercy that whispers the covenant is not dead. The God who judges is also the God who keeps His promise; the darkness of exile is real, but it is not the end of the story. Out of David's preserved line, in the fullness of time, would come the King whose kingdom cannot be shaken. **2 Kings 全书完成。** From Solomon's temple to its burning, from David's throne to a captive king's restored table, the books of Kings have shown both the terrible cost of forsaking the LORD and the unbreakable faithfulness of the God who, even in judgment, keeps the lamp of David burning toward the dawn of redemption.
